news_summary: Today's health news highlights remarkable athletic achievement, the life-extending benefits of exercise, and groundbreaking DNA research reshaping our understanding of human origins.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité santé d'aujourd'hui met en avant des exploits sportifs remarquables, les bienfaits de l'exercice physique pour prolonger la vie, ainsi que des recherches révolutionnaires sur l'ADN qui redéfinissent notre compréhension des origines de l'humanité.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de salud de hoy destacan un logro deportivo extraordinario, los beneficios del ejercicio para alargar la vida y una investigación pionera sobre el ADN que está transformando nuestra comprensión de los orígenes del ser humano.

articles.title: Sawe smashes two-hour mark to 'move goalposts for marathon running'
articles.summary: Sabastian Sawe makes history at the London Marathon as the first person to run a sub-two-hour marathon in a competitive race.
articles.content:
Sabastian Sawe made history at the London Marathon by becoming the first athlete to run a sub-two-hour marathon in a competitive race.

The 31-year-old Kenyan crossed the line to win in one hour 59 minutes 30 seconds, more than one minute faster than the late Kelvin Kiptum's previous record of 2:00:35, set in 2023.

The great Eliud Kipchoge became the first man to run a marathon in under two hours in 2019, but that was not record-eligible as it was held under controlled conditions.

Already on world record pace as he crossed the halfway mark in 1:00:29, Sawe was able to speed up over the second half of the race to run even faster than Kipchoge's time.

Sawe made his decisive move before the final 10km, with only debutant Yomif Kejelcha able to cover his surge off the front.

Remarkably, Kejelcha, making his marathon debut, became the second man to run under two hours in race conditions, finishing runner-up in 1:59:41.

Half marathon world record holder Jacob Kiplimo also crossed the line faster than Kiptum's former record, completing the podium in 2:00:28.

Sawe, speaking on BBC TV, said: "I am feeling good. I am so happy. It is a day to remember for me."

"We started the race well. Approaching finishing the race, I was feeling strong. Finally reaching the finish line, I saw the time, and I was so excited."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant athletic achievement with broad inspirational impact, as Sabastian Sawe became the first person to run a sub-two-hour marathon in a competitive race, breaking previous records and demonstrating human endurance limits. This milestone has meaningful relevance for sports, health, and human potential, with clear context and details about the event and its significance.
articles.personality_title: Sabastian Sawe breaks two-hour barrier in competitive marathon for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Running a marathon in under two hours has long been seen as a major challenge. The great runner Eliud Kipchoge first broke this barrier in 2019, but his run was not in a normal race, so it did not count as an official record.

**What happened** – At the London Marathon, Kenyan athlete Sabastian Sawe ran the race in 1 hour 59 minutes and 30 seconds on April 21, 2024. This made him the first person to officially finish a competitive marathon in less than two hours. He won the race by more than a minute ahead of the previous fastest official time. Another runner, Yomif Kejelcha, also finished under two hours in his first marathon, coming in second.

**Impact** – Sawe’s achievement shows that running a marathon under two hours is possible during real competition, not just special events. This pushes the limits of what athletes can do and sets a new standard for marathon running. It also inspires runners and sports fans worldwide by showing how training and determination can lead to new records.

**What's next step** – Sawe’s success may encourage more athletes to aim for sub-two-hour marathons in official races. It could lead to faster marathon times and new training methods. Organizers might also see more interest in marathon competitions as the sport reaches new milestones.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Sabastian Sawe became the first runner to officially finish a competitive marathon in under two hours, setting a new milestone in long-distance running.
articles.personality_title_fr: Sabastian Sawe franchit la barre des deux heures dans un marathon officiel pour la première fois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Courir un marathon en moins de deux heures est un défi majeur depuis longtemps. Le célèbre coureur Eliud Kipchoge a d'abord franchi cette limite en 2019, mais sa course n'était pas une compétition officielle, donc ce n'était pas un record.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Lors du marathon de Londres, l'athlète kényan Sabastian Sawe a terminé la course en 1 heure 59 minutes et 30 secondes le 21 avril 2024. Il est ainsi devenu le premier à finir officiellement un marathon compétitif en moins de deux heures. Il a gagné la course avec plus d'une minute d'avance sur le précédent meilleur temps officiel. Un autre coureur, Yomif Kejelcha, a aussi terminé en moins de deux heures lors de son premier marathon, arrivant deuxième.

**Impact** – La performance de Sawe montre qu’il est possible de courir un marathon sous les deux heures en compétition réelle, pas seulement lors d’événements spéciaux. Cela repousse les limites des athlètes et fixe une nouvelle norme pour le marathon. Cela inspire aussi les coureurs et les fans de sport dans le monde entier.

**Prochaine étape** – Le succès de Sawe pourrait encourager plus d’athlètes à viser des marathons sous les deux heures en compétition officielle. Cela pourrait conduire à des temps plus rapides et de nouvelles méthodes d’entraînement. Les organisateurs pourraient aussi voir un intérêt accru pour les marathons.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Sabastian Sawe est devenu le premier coureur à terminer officiellement un marathon compétitif en moins de deux heures, établissant un nouveau record en course de fond.
articles.personality_title_es: Sabastian Sawe rompe la barrera de las dos horas en un maratón competitivo por primera vez
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Correr un maratón en menos de dos horas ha sido un gran desafío durante mucho tiempo. El gran corredor Eliud Kipchoge fue el primero en romper esta barrera en 2019, pero su carrera no fue una competencia oficial, por lo que no contó como récord.

**Qué pasó** – En el Maratón de Londres, el atleta keniano Sabastian Sawe completó la carrera en 1 hora 59 minutos y 30 segundos el 21 de abril de 2024. Así, se convirtió en el primer corredor en terminar oficialmente un maratón competitivo en menos de dos horas. Ganó la carrera con más de un minuto de ventaja sobre el récord oficial anterior. Otro corredor, Yomif Kejelcha, también terminó en menos de dos horas en su primer maratón, quedando en segundo lugar.

**Impacto** – El logro de Sawe demuestra que es posible correr un maratón en menos de dos horas en una competencia real, no solo en eventos especiales. Esto empuja los límites de los atletas y establece un nuevo estándar para el maratón. También inspira a corredores y aficionados al deporte en todo el mundo.

**Próximo paso** – El éxito de Sawe podría motivar a más atletas a intentar maratones oficiales bajo las dos horas. Esto podría llevar a tiempos más rápidos y nuevas formas de entrenamiento. Los organizadores podrían ver un aumento en el interés por las competiciones de maratón.

**Mensaje en una frase** – Sabastian Sawe se convirtió en el primer corredor en terminar oficialmente un maratón competitivo en menos de dos horas, marcando un nuevo hito en las carreras de larga distancia.
articles.image_prompt: A dynamic, flowing painting of three abstract silhouettes made of vibrant, intertwined ribbons racing along a winding marathon path, with one figure breaking through a glowing finish line shaped like a clock striking two hours, set against a softly lit sunrise background in warm, earthy tones.

articles.title: This one change to your exercise routine could add years to your life
articles.summary: Mixing up your workouts might be the real secret to a longer life. Long-term research tracking over 100,000 people for more than three decades suggests that doing a variety of physical activities—rather than just more of the same—can significantly lower the risk of death. Interestingly, the benefits don’t keep rising endlessly; they seem to level off after a certain point, hinting at a “sweet spot” of activity.
articles.content:
Regularly engaging in a variety of physical activities could be one of the most effective ways to extend lifespan, according to research published in the open access journal BMJ Medicine. The findings suggest the relationship is not simply about doing more exercise. Instead, benefits appear to level off after a certain point, indicating there may be an optimal amount of activity.

The results also show that variety itself matters. People who participate in different types of physical activity tend to have a lower risk of death regardless of how much total exercise they do. Still, the researchers emphasize that staying active overall remains important.

Why Exercise Variety Matters

Physical activity has long been linked to better physical and mental health, along with a reduced risk of death. However, it has been less clear whether certain types of exercise offer unique advantages, or whether mixing activities provides additional benefits beyond total volume.

To investigate this, researchers analyzed data from two major long-term studies: the Nurses' Health Study (121,700 female participants) and the Health Professionals Follow-Up Study (51,529 male participants). These studies tracked participants for more than 30 years, with regular updates on lifestyle, health history, and exercise habits collected every 2 years through questionnaires.

Decades of Data on Movement and Lifestyle

Participants reported a wide range of physical activities over time. Since 1986, this included walking, jogging, running, cycling (including stationary machines), lap swimming, rowing or callisthenics, tennis and squash or racquetball.

Later surveys added more detail, covering weight training or resistance exercise; lower intensity exercise, such as yoga, stretching, and toning; vigorous tasks like lawn mowing; moderate outdoor work such as maintenance and gardening; and heavy outdoor work like digging and chopping.

Participants also reported how many flights of stairs they climbed daily, based on the estimate that each flight takes 8 seconds to ascend.

The analysis included 111,467 participants for total physical activity and 111,373 participants for activity variety. To measure activity levels, researchers used MET scores, calculated by multiplying the average time spent on each activity (in hours/week) by its MET value. METs indicate how much more energy an activity uses compared to resting.

Activity Levels, Habits, and Health Profiles

Across both groups, individuals could report up to 11 or 13 different activities depending on the study. Walking was the most common form of leisure exercise, while men were more likely than women to jog or run.

People who reported higher overall activity levels were generally healthier. They were less likely to smoke or have high blood pressure or high cholesterol. They also tended to have a lower body weight (lower BMI), eat healthier diets, drink alcohol, maintain stronger social connections, and take part in a wider range of activities.

Exercise and Risk of Death Over 30 Years

During more than three decades of follow-up, 38,847 participants died, including 9901 from cardiovascular disease, 10,719 from cancer, and 3,159 from respiratory disease.

Higher levels of physical activity, along with most individual types of exercise except swimming, were linked to a lower risk of death from any cause. However, the relationship was not linear. The benefits of total activity appeared to level off after about 20 weekly MET hours, suggesting there may be a point beyond which additional activity provides less added benefit.

Which Activities Were Linked to Lower Risk

Walking showed one of the strongest associations, with those who walked the most having a 17% lower risk of death compared with those who walked the least. Climbing stairs was linked to a 10% lower risk.

Other activities were also associated with reduced risk when comparing the least active to the most active participants. Tennis, squash, or racquetball were linked to a 15% lower risk. Rowing or callisthenics showed a 14% reduction. Weight training or resistance exercises and running were each linked to a 13% lower risk. Jogging was associated with an 11% reduction, while cycling showed a smaller 4% decrease.

The Added Benefit of Exercise Variety

Engaging in a wider range of activities was linked to even greater benefits. After accounting for total exercise levels, participants who performed the most diverse set of activities had a 19% lower risk of death from all causes.

They also showed a 13-41% lower risk of death from cardiovascular disease, cancer, respiratory disease, and other causes compared with those who engaged in fewer types of activity.

Study Limitations and What It Means

This research is observational, which means it cannot prove cause and effect. The researchers also point out several limitations. Physical activity was self-reported rather than directly measured, which may affect accuracy.

In addition, MET scores were calculated under the assumption that participants were fully engaged in each activity, and the lack of detailed information on intensity could have led to some misclassification of energy use. The study population was also mostly White, which may limit how widely the findings apply.

Even so, the researchers conclude: "Overall, these data support the notion that long term engagement in multiple types of physical activity may help extend the lifespan."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant long-term research study involving over 100,000 participants that shows engaging in a variety of physical activities can substantially lower the risk of death and extend lifespan. The findings have broad public health implications, encouraging diverse exercise habits for longevity. The article provides detailed context, data, and specific activity benefits, making it a substantive and inspiring health-related discovery.
articles.personality_title: Varied exercise routines linked to longer life, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – A large study followed over 100,000 people for more than 30 years to understand how exercise affects lifespan. It looked at not just how much people exercised but also the types of activities they did.

**What happened** – Researchers analyzed data from two long-term health studies involving nurses and health professionals. Participants reported their weekly physical activities, which included walking, running, cycling, swimming, tennis, and more. The study found that people who did a variety of exercises had a lower risk of death compared to those who stuck to just one type.

**Impact** – The study showed that mixing different kinds of physical activities can reduce the chance of dying from any cause by up to 19%. Walking, climbing stairs, tennis, and weight training were especially helpful. Interestingly, the benefits of exercise increased only up to a certain point, after which doing more didn’t add much extra advantage.

**What's next step** – Researchers suggest that people should aim to include different types of exercises in their routines rather than focusing on just one. Future studies might explore how exercise variety works in different groups of people and how intensity affects health benefits.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Doing many kinds of physical activities regularly can help people live longer than doing just one type of exercise.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une routine d'exercice variée liée à une vie plus longue, selon une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Une grande étude a suivi plus de 100 000 personnes pendant plus de 30 ans pour comprendre comment l'exercice influence la durée de vie. Elle a examiné non seulement la quantité d'exercice, mais aussi les types d'activités pratiquées.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les chercheurs ont analysé des données provenant de deux grandes études de santé impliquant des infirmières et des professionnels de santé. Les participants ont rapporté leurs activités physiques hebdomadaires, comprenant la marche, la course, le vélo, la natation, le tennis, et plus encore. L'étude a découvert que les personnes qui pratiquaient une variété d'exercices avaient un risque de décès plus faible que celles qui se limitaient à un seul type.

**Impact** – L'étude a montré que mélanger différents types d'activités physiques peut réduire le risque de décès de toutes causes jusqu'à 19 %. La marche, monter les escaliers, le tennis, et la musculation étaient particulièrement bénéfiques. Fait intéressant, les bienfaits de l'exercice augmentaient seulement jusqu'à un certain point, après quoi faire plus n'apportait pas beaucoup plus d'avantages.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs suggèrent que les gens devraient inclure différents types d'exercices dans leur routine plutôt que de se concentrer sur un seul. Les futures études pourraient explorer comment la variété d'exercice agit dans différents groupes de personnes et comment l'intensité influence les bienfaits pour la santé.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pratiquer régulièrement plusieurs types d'activités physiques peut aider à vivre plus longtemps que de faire un seul type d'exercice.
articles.personality_title_es: Una rutina de ejercicio variada está relacionada con una vida más larga, según estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Un gran estudio siguió a más de 100,000 personas durante más de 30 años para entender cómo el ejercicio afecta la duración de la vida. Se analizó no solo cuánto ejercitaban las personas, sino también qué tipos de actividades realizaban.

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores analizaron datos de dos grandes estudios de salud que incluyeron a enfermeras y profesionales de la salud. Los participantes reportaron sus actividades físicas semanales, que incluían caminar, correr, andar en bicicleta, nadar, tenis y más. El estudio encontró que quienes realizaban una variedad de ejercicios tenían un menor riesgo de muerte que quienes se limitaban a un solo tipo.

**Impacto** – El estudio mostró que combinar diferentes tipos de actividades físicas puede reducir el riesgo de muerte por cualquier causa hasta en un 19 %. Caminar, subir escaleras, jugar tenis y hacer entrenamiento de fuerza fueron especialmente útiles. Curiosamente, los beneficios del ejercicio aumentaban solo hasta cierto punto, después del cual hacer más no añadía mucha ventaja.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores sugieren que las personas deberían incluir diferentes tipos de ejercicios en sus rutinas en lugar de enfocarse en uno solo. Estudios futuros podrían explorar cómo funciona la variedad de ejercicios en diferentes grupos y cómo la intensidad afecta los beneficios para la salud.

**Resumen en una frase** – Hacer varios tipos de actividades físicas regularmente puede ayudar a las personas a vivir más tiempo que hacer solo un tipo de ejercicio.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant, winding path composed of diverse exercise icons—walking shoes, a tennis racket, a bicycle, a set of stairs, and a rowing paddle—intertwined with flowing ribbons representing energy, surrounded by gentle natural tones and soft light symbolizing longevity and vitality.

articles.title: DNA research just rewrote the origin of human species
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new picture of human origins that challenges the long-held idea of a single ancestral population in Africa. By analyzing genetic data from diverse modern African groups—especially the highly distinct Nama people—and comparing it with fossil evidence, researchers found that early humans likely evolved from multiple intermingling populations over hundreds of thousands of years. Rather than a clean split, these groups stayed connected, exchanging genes even after beginning to diverge around 120,000–135,000 years ago.
articles.content:
A sweeping genetic analysis is challenging one of the simplest versions of the human origin story: the idea that all modern humans arose from a single ancestral population in Africa. Instead, the research points to a more intricate beginning, with early human groups spread across Africa, mixing for long stretches of time before some of their differences became visible in the DNA of people living today.

The study, published in Nature in 2023, compared genetic material from present day African populations with fossil evidence from early Homo sapiens populations. The result was a model of human evolution that replaces a clean family tree with something more like a network of deeply connected branches.

A More Complex Beginning in Africa

Scientists broadly agree that Homo sapiens originated in Africa. The harder question is how early human groups separated, moved, reconnected, and shaped one another across the continent.

Brenna Henn, professor of anthropology and the Genome Center at UC Davis and corresponding author of the study, said the uncertainty comes from gaps in both fossils and ancient DNA.

"This uncertainty is due to limited fossil and ancient genomic data, and to the fact that the fossil record does not always align with expectations from models built using modern DNA," she said. "This new research changes the origin of species."

The work was co led by Henn and Simon Gravel of McGill University. Their team tested several competing ideas about human evolution and migration in Africa, drawing from models proposed in paleoanthropology and genetics. The analysis included genome data from southern, eastern and western Africa.

The Nama Genomes Added a Key Clue

A major part of the study came from 44 newly sequenced genomes from modern Nama individuals in southern Africa. The Nama are an Indigenous population known for carrying unusually high levels of genetic diversity compared with many other living groups.

Researchers collected saliva samples from people in their villages between 2012 and 2015, while participants were going about daily life. Those samples helped the team examine whether human origins fit a single source model or something broader and more interconnected.

The best fitting model suggested that the earliest population split among early humans still detectable in living people happened roughly 120,000 to 135,000 years ago. Before that split, two or more weakly differentiated Homo populations had been exchanging genes for hundreds of thousands of years.

Even after the split, movement and mating continued between these early groups. The researchers describe this as a weakly structured stem, meaning the roots of modern humans were not one isolated population, but a loose set of connected populations with ongoing gene flow.

Not One Branch, But a Network

That network like model may explain human genetic diversity better than older models, according to the authors. Instead of needing to assume major contributions from an unknown archaic hominin population in Africa, the model shows how patterns in modern DNA could have emerged from structure within ancestral human populations themselves.

"We are presenting something that people had never even tested before," Henn said of the research. "This moves anthropological science significantly forward."

Co-author Tim Weaver, a UC Davis professor of anthropology who studies early human fossils, said the results shift how scientists should think about older explanations.

"Previous more complicated models proposed contributions from archaic hominins, but this model indicates otherwise," he said.

Weaver contributed comparative fossil expertise to the study, helping connect genetic models with what early human remains looked like.

What This Means for Ancient Fossils

The model also has consequences for how scientists interpret the fossil record. According to the authors, only 1 to 4% of genetic differentiation among living human populations can be traced to variation between these ancestral stem populations.

Because the early branches continued mixing, they were probably similar in appearance. That means fossils with very different physical traits (such as Homo naledi) are unlikely to represent lineages that directly contributed to the evolution of Homo sapiens, the authors said.

In other words, the roots of humanity may have been geographically and genetically widespread, but not necessarily divided into sharply different human forms. The deeper picture is one of movement, contact, and repeated mixing across Africa.

Later Research Adds More Depth

Work published after the 2023 study has continued to show how important African genomic diversity is for understanding human origins. A 2024 Nature Ecology & Evolution study reported 9,000 years of genetic continuity in southernmost Africa, highlighting the region's long and unusually deep human population history.

A later Nature study analyzed genomes from 28 ancient southern African individuals dated between 10,200 and 150 years before present. That work found that ancient southern Africans carried genetic variation outside the range seen in living people and identified Homo sapiens specific variants that may shed light on adaptation and evolution within Africa.

Together, these findings strengthen a bigger message: human origins were not a single spark in one place. They were shaped by many populations, deep African diversity, and long periods of connection across the continent.

Additional co-authors of the 2023 study include Aaron Ragsdale, University of Wisconsin, Madison; Elizabeth Atkinson, Baylor College of Medicine; and Eileen Hoal and Marlo Möller, Stellenbosch University, South Africa.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major scientific breakthrough in understanding human origins through genetic research, revealing a complex network of interrelated ancestral populations in Africa rather than a single source. This discovery has broad significance for anthropology, genetics, and human history, offering a deeper and more accurate picture of human evolution that enriches our knowledge of humanity's past. The study is well-detailed and based on extensive genetic and fossil evidence, fulfilling criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: New DNA study reveals human origins from interconnected African populations
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long believed that all modern humans come from one single group in Africa. This idea was based on fossils and some genetic studies, but there were still many questions about how early humans lived and moved across the continent.

**What happened** – In 2023, researchers led by Brenna Henn from UC Davis and Simon Gravel from McGill University studied DNA from many African groups, including 44 genomes from the Nama people in southern Africa. They compared this genetic data with early human fossils. Their analysis showed that early humans did not come from just one group but from several groups that stayed connected and shared genes for hundreds of thousands of years, even after starting to separate around 120,000 to 135,000 years ago.

**Impact** – This finding changes how scientists understand human evolution. Instead of a simple family tree with one root, human origins look more like a network of branches that stayed linked. It explains why humans today have so much genetic diversity without needing to guess about unknown ancient human species mixing in. It also helps explain fossil differences by showing early human groups were more alike than previously thought.

**What's next step** – Researchers will use this new model to study more ancient DNA and fossils across Africa. Ongoing research, like studies from 2024, is already showing long-lasting genetic connections in southern Africa. This work will deepen knowledge about how humans adapted and evolved in different regions over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New DNA research shows that modern humans evolved from many interconnected populations in Africa, not just one single ancestral group.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude ADN révèle que les origines humaines viennent de populations africaines interconnectées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques pensaient depuis longtemps que tous les humains modernes descendent d’un seul groupe en Afrique. Cette idée était basée sur des fossiles et certaines études génétiques, mais beaucoup de questions restaient sur la vie et les déplacements des premiers humains à travers le continent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2023, des chercheurs dirigés par Brenna Henn de l’université de Californie à Davis et Simon Gravel de l’université McGill ont étudié l’ADN de nombreux groupes africains, dont 44 génomes de la population Nama en Afrique du Sud. Ils ont comparé ces données génétiques avec des fossiles humains anciens. Leur analyse a montré que les premiers humains ne venaient pas d’un seul groupe, mais de plusieurs groupes qui sont restés connectés et ont échangé leurs gènes pendant des centaines de milliers d’années, même après avoir commencé à se séparer il y a environ 120 000 à 135 000 ans.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la façon dont les scientifiques comprennent l’évolution humaine. Au lieu d’un arbre généalogique simple avec une seule origine, les origines humaines ressemblent plus à un réseau de branches reliées. Cela explique la grande diversité génétique humaine actuelle sans avoir besoin d’imaginer des espèces humaines anciennes inconnues. Cela aide aussi à comprendre les différences entre fossiles en montrant que les groupes humains anciens étaient plus semblables qu’on ne le pensait.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont utiliser ce nouveau modèle pour étudier plus d’ADN ancien et de fossiles à travers l’Afrique. Des recherches en cours, comme celles de 2024, montrent déjà des liens génétiques durables en Afrique australe. Ces travaux approfondiront la connaissance de la manière dont les humains se sont adaptés et ont évolué dans différentes régions au fil du temps.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle recherche ADN montre que les humains modernes ont évolué à partir de nombreuses populations interconnectées en Afrique, pas d’un seul groupe ancestral.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio de ADN revela que los orígenes humanos provienen de poblaciones africanas interconectadas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos pensaron que todos los humanos modernos provenían de un solo grupo en África. Esta idea se basaba en fósiles y algunos estudios genéticos, pero quedaban muchas preguntas sobre cómo vivían y se movían los primeros humanos en el continente.

**Qué pasó** – En 2023, investigadores dirigidos por Brenna Henn de UC Davis y Simon Gravel de la Universidad McGill estudiaron el ADN de varios grupos africanos, incluyendo 44 genomas de la población Nama en el sur de África. Compararon estos datos genéticos con fósiles humanos antiguos. Su análisis mostró que los primeros humanos no provenían de un solo grupo, sino de varios grupos que permanecieron conectados y compartieron genes durante cientos de miles de años, incluso después de comenzar a separarse hace unos 120,000 a 135,000 años.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia cómo los científicos entienden la evolución humana. En lugar de un árbol genealógico simple con una sola raíz, los orígenes humanos parecen más una red de ramas conectadas. Esto explica la gran diversidad genética actual sin necesidad de suponer la mezcla con especies humanas antiguas desconocidas. También ayuda a entender las diferencias en los fósiles mostrando que los grupos humanos antiguos eran más parecidos de lo que se pensaba.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán este nuevo modelo para estudiar más ADN antiguo y fósiles en África. Investigaciones recientes, como las de 2024, ya muestran conexiones genéticas duraderas en el sur de África. Este trabajo profundizará el conocimiento sobre cómo los humanos se adaptaron y evolucionaron en distintas regiones a lo largo del tiempo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Nueva investigación de ADN muestra que los humanos modernos evolucionaron a partir de muchas poblaciones interconectadas en África, no de un solo grupo ancestral.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate network of interconnected, glowing DNA strands weaves across a warm-toned African landscape at sunset, with softly silhouetted outlines of diverse human skull fossils embedded subtly within the strands, symbolizing the complex, intertwined origins and genetic diversity of early Homo sapiens across Africa.
